summary: P030 is a second-year financial maths student who has undergone a significant transformation in his relationship with money and food delivery since starting university. He presents as someone who learned financial discipline the hard way - after spending freely in first year and experiencing financial constraints, he now approaches spending with careful consideration and views himself as more financially savvy than his peers. His delivery ordering has evolved from 2-3 times per week in first year to 1-2 times currently, reflecting this newfound financial consciousness. Despite this restraint, he maintains a clear framework for when delivery feels justified - primarily as a reward or celebration after achievements, busy days, or rugby victories. His approach to ordering is methodical and value-driven, consistently comparing apps and building baskets on both Deliveroo and Uber Eats to ensure he gets the best deal. He lives with five other male housemates in what appears to be a more harmonious living situation than his previous year, though the group dynamics around food remain individualistic - they prefer to order separately rather than coordinate group orders due to differing preferences.
personality_notes: Articulate and reflective communicator who speaks in measured, thoughtful responses. Shows strong analytical thinking (fitting for a maths student) and demonstrates genuine self-awareness about his behavioral changes. Comes across as responsible and mature, with a clear sense of personal growth and learning from past mistakes.
food_relationship: Views delivery as a calculated reward system within a broader framework of financial responsibility and cooking pragmatism.

key_moments.description: Explains his financial transformation from spending freely to being more careful with money
key_moments.significance: Reveals the learning process that now drives his delivery behavior - shows this isn't just theoretical budgeting but learned from real financial consequences

key_moments.description: Describes delivery as something he orders after feeling like he's 'earned it' - after good uni days, exams, rugby wins
key_moments.significance: Shows delivery functions as a reward system tied to achievement and effort, not just convenience

key_moments.description: During live order, confirms he always compares apps to 'get the best deal possible'
key_moments.significance: Demonstrates that his stated value-conscious behavior is genuine - he actually does the comparison work he claims

what_not_said: Never mentioned any financial support from parents or family
Didn't discuss any negative aspects of his current living situation
Avoided specifics about what went wrong financially in first year
No mention of any food restrictions, dietary preferences, or health considerations
initial_hypotheses: His financial discipline may be more fragile than he presents - the reward framework could easily expand if circumstances change
The methodical app comparison behavior suggests he may be over-optimizing small purchases as a way to feel in control of spending
His individual ordering preference despite living with 5 housemates may indicate social dynamics or personal boundaries around food/money
The 'earned it' mentality could make him particularly responsive to achievement-based or milestone marketing
questions_for_coding: How does his reward-based ordering framework compare to other participants' trigger patterns?
Is his app comparison behavior typical among financially conscious students?
How does his living situation (6 male housemates) influence his food and delivery patterns compared to other housing arrangements?
Does his financial transformation narrative appear in other participants who've been at university longer?
